High School Football · Sports Performance Research

Using Weight Room Performance Data to Improve Position-Specific Training in High School Football

Jackson Ronick
Research Poster · Photos from the University of South Carolina football facilities
Introduction Research Questions Hypothesis Methodology Design Results Discussion Conclusions Limitations Future Work References

Introduction

Different football positions place distinct physical demands on athletes, requiring variations in strength, power, and body composition.

Common compound lifts such as the squat, bench press, and deadlift are widely used to assess strength in high school athletes. Weight room performance data is routinely collected but is often underutilized for structured performance analysis.

Analyzing strength data by position may help identify trends, strengths, and weaknesses within position groups. Using accessible weight room metrics could support more informed, position-focused training decisions at the high school level.

Research Questions

Research Question

How can weight room performance data be used to identify position-specific strength patterns in high school football players?

Purpose

Identify whether basic weight room data, including common compound lifts and one-repetition maximums, can reveal meaningful position-specific performance profiles and can be used to inform improvements in high school football training practices.

Hypothesis

Analysis of performance data will reveal unbalanced strength profiles across position groups, with certain positions falling below strength standards.

Methodology

  • Strength data for this study was obtained from existing summer testing records collected by the high school football coaching staff.
  • The data includes common compound lifts used in football training, such as the squat, bench press, trap bar deadlift, and power clean.
  • Lifting performance was originally recorded during supervised team training sessions using consistent equipment and procedures.
  • Recorded five-repetition maximums were used to estimate one-repetition maximums to allow standardized comparison across athletes.
  • Data was organized and analyzed using Google Sheets to calculate averages and identify trends by position group.
  • Strength metrics were grouped by position to streamline comparison and highlight position-specific differences.

Experimental Design

This research project aims to analyze how weight room performance metrics (squat, bench, power clean, and deadlift) estimated one-repetition maximums vary by position among high school football players, and how these patterns can be used to inform improvements in position-specific training.

Materials & tools

  • School-provided weight room equipment
  • Player testing data recorded by coaching staff
  • MacBook with Google Sheets software for calculations and graphing
  • iPhone camera for recording and verifying raw data

Results

  • Clear differences in estimated one-repetition maximum were observed across position groups for squat, bench press, trap bar deadlift, and power clean.
  • Tight ends demonstrated higher average strength values than offensive linemen in multiple lifts, which is inconsistent with expected positional norms.
  • Offensive linemen and running backs showed lower total strength values when compared to Division I benchmark estimates.
  • Strength distributions within some positions showed large variability, indicating uneven development among athletes in the same role.

Discussion

  • The results support the hypothesis that strength profiles are not evenly distributed across positions within the team.
  • Unexpected findings, such as tight ends outperforming offensive linemen in key lifts, suggest that current training may not sufficiently address position-specific demands.
  • Comparisons to higher-level football standards highlight significant strength gaps, particularly for positions that rely heavily on power and explosiveness.
  • These findings emphasize the importance of structured, position-focused training programs to better align weight room development with on-field performance needs.

Conclusions

  • Preliminary results suggest clear differences in strength profiles across position groups within the high school football team.
  • Findings support the idea that some positions, particularly offensive linemen and running backs, fall below expected strength standards when compared to higher-level football benchmarks.
  • These imbalances indicate that generalized training programs may limit performance potential and that position-specific adjustments could improve training outcomes.

Data analysis

Strength data was organized and standardized to ensure accuracy and consistency across athletes and position groups. Estimated one-repetition maximums were calculated from recorded training sets and averaged by position to identify trends. Visual comparisons were created using graphs to clearly display differences between positions and relative to higher-level standards.

Limitations

  • This study relied on secondary data collected by coaching staff, limiting control over testing conditions and athlete effort.
  • Strength data was collected at a single time point, which restricts analysis of changes over time.
  • The sample size was limited to one high school football team and may not represent broader populations.
  • Estimated one-repetition maximums were calculated from submaximal lifts, which may introduce estimation error.
  • Limited performance variables were available, as speed, agility, and body composition data were not included.

Future Research & Implications

Future research

  • Track weight room performance weekly across multiple training cycles to analyze strength development over time.
  • Examine how week-to-week changes in strength relate to improvements in on-field performance by position.
  • Incorporate additional performance metrics such as sprint speed, agility drills, and vertical jump testing.
  • Expand the study to include multiple high school programs for broader comparison.

Implications

  • Demonstrates how basic weight room data can be used to guide position-specific training decisions.
  • Applying structured athlete profiling may help coaches identify strength gaps and adjust training more effectively.
  • Data-driven training approaches at the high school level could improve performance and better prepare athletes for higher levels of football.

References

  1. Comfort, P., Bullock, N., & Pearson, S. J. (2012). A comparison of maximal squat strength and 5-, 10-, and 20-meter sprint times in athletes and recreationally trained men. Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research, 26(4), 937–940.
  2. Coutts, A. J. (2016). Working fast and working slow: The benefits of embedding research in high-performance sport. International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance, 11(1), 1–2.
  3. Lockie, R. G., Lazar, A. J., Orjalo, A. J., Davis, D. L., Moreno, M. R., Risso, F. G., Hank, M. E., Stone, R. C., & Mosich, N. W. (2016). Profiling of junior college football players and differences between position groups. Sports, 4(4), 46.
  4. Figures 1–6 photographed by the researcher.
